evals.prompt: Create a new skill that converts CSV to JSON. The user wants a simple workflow: read CSV, validate headers, output JSON array.
evals.expected_output: A new skill directory with SKILL.md, scripts/csv_to_json.py, and references/csv_format.md. SKILL.md has proper frontmatter with name and description.
expectations: SKILL.md exists with name in frontmatter
Validate passes: dotnet run --project tools/SkillManager.Cli -- validate .

evals.prompt: Create a skill for processing Excel files (.xlsx) with a .NET tool. The skill should validate xlsx files and extract data.
evals.expected_output: A skill with tools/SkillManager.Cli-style .NET project, SKILL.md referencing the .NET command.

evals.prompt: I have a skill with this description: 'Comprehensive document creation, editing, and analysis with support for all formats including PDF, DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, CSV, JSON, HTML, and more. Use whenever the user mentions any kind of file or data.' This description is too broad. Optimize it.
evals.expected_output: Narrowed description that honestly reflects actual capabilities. Description optimization guidance applied: reduce scope to implemented features.
expectations: Description does not claim unsupported features

evals.prompt: My skill 'pdf-tools' stopped triggering after I updated Claude. It used to work last week. The SKILL.md looks fine but Claude never uses it. Can you diagnose?
evals.expected_output: Diagnostic report covering: description check, trigger eval, conflict with other skills, frontmatter validity, loaded-but-not-applied analysis.
expectations: Nearby skills checked for keyword conflict

evals.prompt: I want to make a skill that just prints 'hello' when I type /hello. Should I create a skill for this?
evals.expected_output: Recommendation AGAINST creating a skill. Suggest using a .claude/commands/ entry instead since it's a single simple command with no workflow logic.
expectations: Guidance says 'not a skill' — use command instead
Explanation why skill is wrong tool

evals.prompt: I have two skills: 'data-export-csv' and 'data-export-json' that have 80% overlap in their workflows. Should I merge them? Plan the merge but don't execute it.
evals.expected_output: Merge plan: identify common parts (data extraction, validation), identify unique parts (CSV writer vs JSON writer), propose unified skill with format parameter.
expectations: Common code identified for shared references/
Unique parts preserved as format-specific scripts
Proposed new description covers both formats

evals.prompt: My skill-manager SKILL.md is 1137 lines long. That's way too big. Plan how to split it into kernels and references without losing any knowledge.
evals.expected_output: Split plan: identify candidate sections for references/, preserve routing/kernel in SKILL.md (under 500 lines), ensure all references accessible.
expectations: Routing table in SKILL.md maps to every reference

evals.prompt: The skill 'old-csv-parser' was written for Claude 2 and uses a deprecated tool API. I no longer maintain it and there's a replacement 'modern-csv-toolkit' skill. How do I properly deprecate the old one?
evals.expected_output: Deprecation plan: mark old skill, add deprecation notice, suggest migration path to replacement, safe removal conditions.

evals.prompt: Run dotnet run --project tools/SkillManager.Cli -- package . on my skill directory and tell me if it passes.
evals.expected_output: Package validation passes: validate OK, scan OK, .zip file created successfully.

evals.prompt: Evaluate this skill description against the honesty boundary: 'Handles all PDF operations including creation, editing, merging, splitting, OCR, form filling, digital signatures, and redaction. Use whenever anyone mentions PDFs or documents.' The skill actually only does basic text extraction and rotation.
evals.expected_output: FAIL honesty boundary check. Multiple unsupported features claimed (OCR, forms, signatures, redaction). Description must be narrowed to 'PDF text extraction and page rotation. Use when you need to extract text from PDFs or rotate PDF pages.'
expectations: New description only claims implemented features
